body_txt: A Fairytale Wedding Dress will be pure white unless it has a little bit of pink or something as girly to it. The girliness can be done away with, though, for a bride who wants to convey a sense of purity but also emotional maturity or a widow who is married a second time; all such a woman needs is a dress that's simple, understated, and still strikingly beautiful — and white. Examples can range from the saint-like to a sexually inexperienced woman who wanted to "lose it", to an experienced one who has somehow renewed her sense of purity. Very commonly associated with goddesses and other über female character types. In Western cultures, this trope is surprisingly recent . Before the mid-19th century, wedding dresses weren't expected to be white; a bride would simply wear her nicest dress, though there were regional customs about which colours were appropriate; white was associated with German aristocracy. Queen Victoria wore a fancy white dress when she married Prince Albert, as both were German; since white clothes were particularly expensive to make and maintain, and she was only going to wear it once, this was a demonstration of Victoria's great wealth; naturally, other women soon copied the queen. The tradition of a white wedding dress was further reïnforced during The Golden Age of Hollywood ; since white is the only colour that stands out on black-and-white film, movies in the 1930s and 1940s put brides in white dresses to make it clear they were wearing a special outfit for the occasion, regardless of the sexual experience of the character (or actress). As millions of young women repeatedly saw women wearing white dresses in film, they got the idea they should do the same, and so a tradition was born. The association of white with virginity was only later grafted on, during the 1950s. This trope is uncommon in Eastern cultures, where virginity is associated with red, which is also the traditional colour of wedding dresses, while white is associated with death. In modern times, other colours are sometimes worn, though white is still rare; some who do wear white say it symbolises their death as a daughter and birth as a wife. An increasingly popular custom is for the bride to wear red for the wedding, but change into a white dress for the reception. In Vietnam, notably, white áo dài is associated with young high school female students both due to their color (purity) and their simultaneous concealment and enhancement of feminine features (modest, elegant beauty without showing "excessive skin"). Often overlaps with Sundress of Innocence . Subtrope of White Is Pure . This is of course the basis of the Blood-Splattered Wedding Dress trope. Compare Gold and White Are Divine and True Blue Femininity . May overlap with Ethereal White Dress if the character died innocent or at a young age, or at least is connected to a creepy supernatural phenomenon. Contrast with the Lady in Red (a polar opposite, more associated with lust) and Widow's Weeds (black and dark clothes associated with widowhood).
